column32: 19th century ghost town whose mines worked the west end of the same 2.5 km long vein system as Tatasí (on the east end of the W-E trending veins). Silver mining started already in the 16th century. Massive miargyrite was the main silver ore mineral in some orebodies. Dacitic stock up to 4,500 meters elevation, intruded Ordovician shales and Tertiary red sandstones.meters elevation, intruded Ordovician shales and Tertiary red sandstones.

column30: andorite', arsenopyrite, boulangerite, bournonite, cassiterite, chalcopyrite, diaphorite, fizélyite, franckeite, 'freibergite subgroup', galena, hocartite, jamesonite, miargyrite, native silver, owyheeite, pyrargyrite, pyrite, quartz, ramdohrite, siderite, sphalerite, stannite, teallite, 'tetrahedrite subgroup'
column32: Active (2019?) Zn-Pb-Sn-Ag mine between the Animas and Chocaya mines, on the same vein system. Sometimes considered to be part of the Animas mine. NB: The Animas, Siete Suyos, Oploca, and Chocaya mines all worked the same system of veins and have very similar mineralogies. For this reason it is generally impossible to tell which mine a specimen actually came from, and "errors" are common both on collectors' labels as well as in the scientific literature, so many collectors just refer to it generically as the "Chocaya-Animas" mine.

column32: Underground salt mine previously worked in large part for cattle in the lowland ranches of eastern Bolivia. Now mostly replaced by cheaper surface salt from the Salar de Uyuni. The salt beds here are the largest known underground halite deposits in Bolivia, extending in a 70km long belt extending north and south of town of Entre Rios.
(Coordinates given are for a "Guadalupe" Sb-Bi mine in Chayanta province, which is probably related to the Chayanta "Guadalupe Nuevo" mine?)

"In the mid-1970’s, Paula and I became friends with Jean Bandy, spending numerous week-ends with her and other friends. As she was trying to decide what to do with the collection, after having been rebuffed by Vince Manson and the American Museum, she had boxes of minerals that Mark had packed away that she needed to open up and sort. As we went through this process, she would occasionally offer me a gift of a specimen. On one fine Saturday, we opened up one box and we unwrapped several specimens of black sphalerite crystals on large octahedral pyrite crystals. I had noticed one of these in the main collection labeled Ubina, Bolivia. As we looked at these specimens, I asked Jean if she remembered a mine name. She laughed, and after qualifying her answer that since all of the people involved had long since died, she told me they used to refer to it as Mr. Pickering’s Mistress’ mine. Mark Bandy was serving as a mine superintendent at one of the mines Mr. Pickering oversaw. At the time, it was common knowledge that not only did Mr. Pickering have a mistress but that she was better paid than any of his superintendents, including Mark. Anyway, the mine where the pyrites came from was one in which Mr. Pickering had advised his mistress to invest some of her income. Jean could not remember any other name for the mine other than the one Mr. Pickering’s staff/spouses had given it. She gave me three specimens from that box and I so enjoyed the story that I am the one who has perpetuated this mine name."
A silver mine that worked Ag-rich sulphosalts.
